rationale: Title Y matches Europace house style more precisely: it uses the journal's full formal name ('European Heart Rhythm Association') in the standard survey-title format and reads with clean, professional clarity. Title X is highly informative — naming the therapeutic spectrum from DOAC switching to LAA closure and specifying 196 physicians — but the result is an overlong, clause-heavy string that feels journalistically cluttered rather than scholarly. Europace titles favour concise, structured phrasing; X's inline enumeration ('from first-line ... to last-resort') and the embedded 'EHRA survey of 196 European physicians' tacked at the end hurt flow and readability. Y sacrifices some specificity but gains in elegance, journal alignment, and immediate appeal to the typical Europace reader.
loser_weakness: Title X is too long and structurally unwieldy; the embedded enumeration of the treatment spectrum and the parenthetical sample size clutter the title and deviate from Europace's preference for concise, cleanly structured manuscript titles.
